transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hey, how's it going?
Yeah, pretty good.
Are you from the Houston area, Stewart?
I saw that you went to Rice.
I'm in Houston right now.
I'm not from there, but I lived there for like six years of college plus post-grad.
Okay, nice.
So you got to see what the city's all about, and summer in New York, it's nothing compared to a summer in Houston, as you know.
They're pretty similar, though.
Yeah, that's right.
Cool.
Well, nice to meet you both.
My name is Tyler Pavlas, based in Houston, and the founding account executive here at GrowthX.
So connected with Kyle and got all the context from your history with us and really, you know, here to use this time in whatever way would be helpful to you both.
So I'll let you provide more context and we'll go from there.
Cool, yeah.
So we use GrowthX maybe six...
months ago now, and I think we used you guys for about a year.
I think at the time, growthx was more of a service.
It sounds like you guys are starting to build a platform.
I think we, at the time, we liked the service.
We felt like we got a lot of volume of content out there.
Um, ultimately, why we churned is that we just didn't see the impact in terms of meetings booked.
Like, there wasn't many people who went, blog, book a meeting, sign up.
Um, now where we're at, uh, we're starting to see more inbound where they mention, uh, an LLM, like ChatGPT, as a source.
Um, and so, we want to double down on that.
Um.
So we're looking at doing it ourself with Aero Ops.
And then we're also interested in more of like a managed service.
It's like, hey, like we want to spend up this many articles per month.
We want to have this many content refreshes per month.
And then you guys go and execute.
So that's the high level context.
I think we have more granular questions, but I guess like where are you guys at right now?
Like how much of it is platform versus services?
Yeah, for sure.
At this time, it's all still service driven.
But in the next probably two months, clients will be able to access the platform.
They'll be able to access it more for visibility around, you know, a lot of the like things we were already doing, but in different tools.
So like your content OS that was in Airtable.
was really
You could see the assignments that are coming up, like the activity log in the platform, the LLM visibility and analytics.
There's a part of the platform that you'd have access to to track different queries and see how you're performing over time.
That's where we're currently at.
But we've done a lot to revamp the way that we partner.
We've got like this two-phased approach now that, you know, starts strategy sprint, moves into growth execution.
You can obviously elaborate, but don't want to like over-answer the question that you asked.
Cool.
That makes sense.
Okay.
Yeah.
Like that's what we're looking for.
Like I think when we started looking at Aerofs, essentially like similar price to GrowthFax from what we paid previously.
Obviously like a little cheaper, but still kind of in the ballpark.
But we have to do everything ourselves.
So that's one of the reasons.
Yeah, for sure.
I guess, how are you thinking about, like, build versus buy here?

You know, like, I think you could get to similar outcomes either way, but if speed is important, if being able to adapt the workflows that you build in air ops over time to new models is important, you probably have a lot of, you know, like, human capital and time spent maintaining what you create.
So I guess help me kind of just better navigate, like, how you're thinking about the decision, and we can go deeper on, you know, what growthx would give you guys that the other option might not.
Yeah, so I'd say, like, where we currently set, we have a strong preference for buy.
I think with build, like, it'll be a lot of time, and I think with something like air ops, like,
It consumes a lot of credits.
Like, it doesn't seem like the trade-off between building and their, like, it kind of feels like building and buying right now, right?
Yeah.
We could just buy.
So that's kind of how we're thinking about it.

But I think, like, long-term, we would love, I think previously when we were working with GrowthX, I think it's, like, kind of what you face when you work with any external, like, kind of consulting agency.
Like, it's hard to get them to care as much about your business as, like, somebody who's NC.
And so I feel like we kind of got to a point where, like, we would have to be more hands-on and prescriptive to potentially do more, like, creative.
So I think that's like one thing we'd want to get clarity with is like who will we be working with on a day-to-day.
Also like pricing to see if it's somewhere to what we were previously doing because if not, that'll probably change the equation.
And then I also want to understand like what do we own like after we're done with the growthx engagement.
I think like long, long term, I would love to be in a spot where we do have somebody in-house who's just focused on this all day.
And so I view growthx or potentially air ops as like a way to get coverage in the near term and then potentially train that person in the future, right?
Like they join and then like maybe there's a world in which like we have that person, we have growthx, we have air ops.
I know RAMP does that today, so it's not completely out of the question, but I would want GrowthX to help with like training somebody, showing them the system, all that stuff.
So I know I threw a lot at you.
What do you think?
No, it's great.
I love it.
The context is all helpful and relevant, and I think we could provide what you're looking for.
So I just need to spend some time in this conversation, answering those questions, and showing you more about how we've evolved with our partnership model.
Before I do that, Harrison, I guess, how involved were you when the team was using GrowthX the first go-round, and how much context do you have?
So I can speak to that for you.
So I actually joined Attention about six months ago, right, when we disengaged from GrowthX.
So I didn't have any experience working with the team.
I've taken a little bit of time to try to learn Aerofs in the past.
A weeks, but as Stu mentioned, just like with our preference for buy, and we don't necessarily have some on the team that can be dedicated fully to SEO and AEO.

I was mainly just trying to learn the basics of AirOps to see if there's a way that we could like fit this into my or someone else's on the team's like core responsibilities without devoting too much time to it.
But, yeah, does that answer your question?
Yeah, definitely.
That helps.
For more context, I'd view Harrison as like the owner and day-to-day POC if we were to engage.
And then, yeah, we'd want him to be, you know, working with your team day-to-day, like being prescriptive, getting the most out of the engagement.
So, yeah, just some context.
Makes perfect sense.
The last question I've got is really...
Just, you mentioned, like, so you guys were with us for, like, six months from what I could see.
And it sounds like it didn't translate to meetings booked.
That was, like, a major reason why you churned.
You were also looking for us to be, like, a bit more creative with our approach or more prescriptive.

Could you just, I guess, like, is what's changed just the realization that, like, okay, like, the foundation to get meetings booked is actually showing up in LLM answers and having a stronger GEO presence.
So, like, I guess is the way that you valued what we were doing before, is it different now in terms of, like, the editorial work that we're doing?
That's part one.
And then part two is just on the creative comment you mentioned.
We can talk about that after.
Yeah, I mean, it seems like AEO is a bigger portion of our inbound now.
So I would say, yeah, that's kind of the catalyst for reengaging.
Um, I feel like when we were doing the Growpacks and...
HMT previously, it wasn't necessarily in the context of AEO.
Like, I feel like there's some pretty basic stuff around, like, how to structure articles that we weren't getting out of GrowthX previously.
Like, just putting, like, the word attention and some of the H1s and H2s and, like, having a FAQ and having, like, really good metadata.
So that's something I would hope would change if we were to re-engage, right?
But I think that's also an area where, like, we probably could have been more prescriptive and on top of things to make sure that was happening in the past.
And so, like, catalyst for re-engagement is having the infrastructure to do that at scale.
So we can either do that with Aerox or GrowthX.
But the reason we're thinking about growth...
Fathom is, you know, we can have some manpower to help with that.
Maybe what I can do is just run through a quick presentation, like show some examples that maybe you haven't seen before.
I'll jump back into the platform so you can see how it's evolved and how the workflows themselves have changed.
And then if I haven't hit any of the questions you've brought up, we can run through those at the end.
And just align on, you know, if you'd maybe want to just chat with Marcel one more time as like a final step before thinking through which approach is better for you.
That sound good?
Yeah.
Let's do it.
I'll just share my screen here.

So, you know, you're spot on, Stu, that like we are really connecting what we do to GEO, because that's where we're seeing the quickest wins, the biggest results, and the step back on like why it matters is...
As I'm sure you know, clicks are starting to disappear, and search visits that come from AI, a lot of them have much higher intent, right?
Sam Rush did a study about how they're 4.4 times more likely to convert, they spend three times as long, and the reality is that the first sales conversation is happening in ChatGPT or other tools.
So, you know, it's no longer like software for a salesperson to be more efficient in a CRM, it's like everything about who that person is.
And that's why like our audience first approach, it really helps us figure out that full surface area, and those main topics that you want to be the answer, right, or that audience around.
So Monograph, they're one of our customers, this example just highlights how SEO and GEO were driving results for them, and a lot of it is because when we create content.
And when it's running through the very complex workflows we've put together, it's hitting all the most important factors.
So deep research, being comprehensive, having your brand, right, as a voice that influences the content, keeping content up to date, et cetera, et cetera.
A lot of what we're seeing, too, is like what you're thinking about.
You can either buy tools or you can buy a partner that's focused on the outcome, which is going to be driving organic traffic.
You can hire an agency that will take on one piece, or you can leverage our approach that is end-to-end partner with AI workflows and then content experts in the loop.
So that's just some high level on what we're thinking in terms of GEO and a little bit of our approach.
Harrison, any questions that you want to ask before I continue to go through?
No, no question to it.
Sounds good.
And part of the differentiation here, it's like the origin story and the folks that built GrowthX.
As I'm sure you know, Stu, know, Marcel has been a content marketing leader his whole career.
He's always believed brands should act more like publishers.
And so he's taken that approach everywhere he's been.
At IBM, he built SecurityIntelligence.com, which was a website that existed for 10 years.
It was like their highest ROI marketing channel.
He took the same approach at HP with AIMinds.com.
had like an expert network of a thousand contributors.
He was the marketing leader at HashiCorp as they grew from 6 million to 100 million.
He was the CMO at Scale when they got acquired by Meta.
And then he took the process that he had at each of those companies and then built AI workflows to try to drive the same results at DeepGram.
And so the DeepGram experiment, which led to some workshops that Marcel started doing, that's what...
Led to GrowthX being created was the reality that, like, it's super hard to build these workflows, to keep them up to date.
It's expensive when you think about all the APIs and tokens that you might use.
And so, you know, that's our approach, right, is using this platform, having experts in the loop.
So you would have a managing editor, a managing director, and a content manager assigned to your account.
So we've got folks that have been SEO experts at some of the companies that you're seeing here, have been editors for websites like G2.com, or even, like, the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch.
So we've scaled, like, a lot since you last were working with us.
We're over 80 employees today, over 10 million in ARR, and working with a lot of awesome brands, maybe some names here that you didn't know we were working with before.
But now we are, and a new model to the way that we run our partnerships.
So today...
We're really focused on two phases.
The first phase is an eight-week strategy sprint.
It would be $20,000 for you guys, so the low ends of the fee.
And we've already done a good amount of work, I think, here in terms of thinking about assignments.
I was checking out, I'm not sure if they were using this with you.
I'm sure they were, but it looks like we've published 145 articles to date and that we've got some ideas that we've thought about for other pieces.
And so we could move fast in the strategy sprint.
We'd create artifacts, which I'm not sure was a part of the process before.
Were we creating artifacts around audience personas, company contacts, when you were engaged with us last?
I think so, yeah.
I mean, there's a lot of contextual documents that were uploaded into the system or the air table or whatever.
So, yeah.
I've...
That Airtable we showed, like, we reviewed that every day or every week.
Yeah.
I mean, how was, like, the collaboration there around the tools we were using, right?
Like, Shared Slack channel, Airtable, like, was that working well for you guys?
Yeah, I would say that was probably, like, one of more impressive parts of the engagement was, like, the content OS, that Airtable.
I think back then, everything was in the context of, like, SEO keyword and potentially, like, not a prompt or something.
So I think that was a little interesting.
I wonder if that changed at all.
Yeah, mean, like, like, weeks for, like, the, like, strategy aspect feels like a lot, given, like, how much context you guys already have on our business.
So I think that's something.
I would like to do something like more custom there, right?
Or maybe a smaller fixed fee, and then we can get to execution faster.
Yeah, and the goal too would be like to give you guys the ability to, you know, opt out if you weren't feeling great about the work we were doing in phase one.
So if that meant that we made it four weeks, and you'd have the first three to make a decision on if you didn't want to opt out from phase two, we could look at something like that.
But I agree, if we've done a lot of this work, like there's no need to repeat it.
And a lot of times, you know, with clients, like we publish during the strategy sprint.
So it won't be just strategy.
It's making sure like we're aligned on the context, the artifacts that would feed into our workflows.
Because ultimately, like we're going to want those to be as good as they can get.
To drive results with every piece of content we create.
Cool.
So there would be, yeah, there would.
What would be some like deliverables during that strategy sprint period?
And I feel like there would have to be if we would still be an evaluation and opt-in out, opt-out stage.
Yeah, absolutely, Harrison.
And you guys would be able to keep any of the artifacts that we create.
So I'm just jumping into Engine.
They're in the business travel space.
Their workspace, like they're one of our customers and we've built a lot of artifacts for them.
So for Engine, they had huge like focus on industry verticals.
So like construction, like different verticals had like more business travel.
And, you know, they were bigger targets for the company.
And so what we did here was we took the persona artifact and then we verticalized it.
And what we're doing is we're trying to get inside the mind of your audience, basically.
So like, who are they?
What motivates them?
How do you win their trust?
How do you lose their trust?
What gets them promoted?
The idea is that if we have these artifacts.
agree.
Fathom integrates.
We can also think in terms of the topics that you want to be the expert around, that you want to show up in the answer around.
And it's a combination of these topics that we focus on with the keywords, the search volume and ranking difficulty.
And so it's like hybrid approach of GEO and SEO, where there's absolutely like keyword opportunities that we're going to focus on for you.
But more broadly, it's topics where you want to be the expert that are connected to those specific keyword opportunities and assignments that we would create content around.
It's hard to explain succinctly, but does that provide some color or like understanding around how we would prioritize like the content we create with you guys?
Yeah, that makes sense.
Are you guys using like a...
The ADO platform right now, like, Profound or something like that?
Yeah, so the thing about Profound, like, great tool, but it's really just visibility, right?
Like, there's no execution or action off of those insights.
We partnered with Scrunch today.
Scrunch, very similar functionality to Profound, gives you, you know, the same sort of insights around specific queries that you want to monitor performance.
With, and then we'd be building our own functionality in the platform when we decide to do our own thing, right?
Versus leverage what Scrunch has already created.
We can just give you what Scrunch has already created, like, out of the box today, and that delivers more value than what we're in the process of building ourselves.
Okay, that makes sense.
I guess, like, what's the more important input today?
Is it, like, a prompt?
Is
Man, tough question to answer.
I think, you know, it's really having, like, a good understanding of your audience, a good understanding of, like, how you're different and what topics you want to have authority around.
But when we actually go in and create assignments, a keyword is, like, the input, right, for the assignment that we would create.
And so, like, this is an interesting example because it's in the supplement space, but it's an example I like because accuracy is, like, really important with this client.
And so this is one where we give some direction.
You know, we're going after, like, a very technical sort of focus in terms of the keyword.
And then we have the company context and the user personas that are the artifacts feeding into this workflow.
And so what happens here is that every step, we are going through hundreds of different steps within.
Within the steps that you see more broadly here.
And so like for this brief creation, we're looking at keyword traffic, organic results, certain ranking factors, and competitor content.
And then all of that informs the brief that you see here, which we then do deep research on.
So generating research questions for that brief, performing deep research on each question, and validating against third-party sources.

As you're continuing through, like, we've built a custom step in this because we had a lot of conversations with their chief medical officer, and they didn't want to be involved in the review process every single time at a certain point.

And so we could ingest those recordings, we could ingest the documentation they put together, and then we had a step where there was like the expert review within the AI workflow that went hand-in-hand with the fact-checking to make sure that, you know, for the draft that we'd written, for every single passage, we're again doing more.
And then doing a rewrite if it's like below a certain confidence interval in terms of the research that we perform here.
So there's a lot that we're doing to like make sure, again, that the content hits those big buckets or AEO.
And that's really kind of like the two-phase approach is we align with you on strategy.
We get artifacts in a great spot.
We're prioritized on the content we want to create.

And then, you know, you guys can be as involved or as uninvolved as you want while we give you the insights, you know, through the platform or through the reporting that we provide prior to the platform being opened up to the clients we work with.
I'm talking about a lot of different things here, but I know we got three minutes left.
What will be like the most helpful kind of final questions to answer before we close this conversation?
Yeah, so I don't think this is to answer on this call, but I think we'd want to get an understanding of like who exactly.
We'll
We'd be working with, and like who would be the day-to-day person.
And then I think on the pricing structure, we'd have to get like the most important thing I'm indexing on is what the monthly fee would be on like a recurring basis once we're kind of up and running.
And then my first glance at what that was kind of proposing was like a 12-month term with like that first four to eight weeks is like a trial period, essentially.
I think what I would love to do is like, hey, we're pretty, like move it.
What we previously had is where we would just renew every three months.
And I think that's more aligned with what we would want to do.
And then I know we spoke about this earlier, but like potentially getting some grace.

On like the strategic fee at the beginning with that, with kind of the justification being like we've already worked together previously, but you guys have like a new platform and it seems like there's some new stuff in there.
So like if there's a compelling case against that, maybe I could lean in a little more on it, but I'll stop negotiating against myself.

I would also be curious, like when you just demoed at the end there, sort of all the steps that go into generating the end article, how much of that were you already using six months ago when we were working with you?
And like how much of that is like a brand new process?
Yeah, good question.
I got here five or six weeks ago.
So the short answer is like, I honestly don't know.
But I think what we can do is I can give you a little bit of clarity here now, and then we should probably set up a follow-up.
Where Marcel can come in and just wax poetic on why now versus the past.
The clarity I can give you now is like we're three to five weeks out booked on these strategy sprint kickoffs.
So we just have a crazy amount of demand right now and we're like limiting our strategy sprint kickoff.
So the quality is as high as possible for every client we work with.
So I think although the team was doing those three-month deals when you first partnered with us, I'm seeing like a three-month deal be approved or be signed since I joined, right?
They're all that 12-month standard and we can be flexible like on different parts of the firms and billing around the contract.
I think if you guys are still interested, right, we should set the meeting up with Marcel so that you could hear from him why the experience would...
Be different than it was previously, but I just want to be like super transparent about what I've seen in today's world versus like the 2024 partnerships we were signing.
Cool.
Yeah, I think that makes sense.
I mean, we still don't have like the monthly fee for the recurring engagement, so it's kind of like the 12 month.
Yeah, no, the monthly fee for you guys, we've been starting clients out at 15, but I know you guys were on nine, so we would put you guys at 12 is what I talked to Kyle about.
Okay.
Got it.
And it'd be nice to know if we were to re-engage like who specifically we would be working with.
Yeah, for sure.
And I think that Marcel could give you clarity on that in the next conversation also.
So I just wouldn't know like from the team who specifically would be on your account.
But yeah, I think, you could schedule that meeting now if you guys think it's a good use of time or I can give you, you know, a day or two to think about it.
Let me know what's best.
Yeah, let's go ahead and schedule it.
Cool.
All right.
Got it.
Looking at Marcel's calendar now.
If we could meet, got a ton of like Series B investor meetings this week.
If we could do next week on Tuesday or Wednesday, that would be perfect.
Yeah, Tuesday is like any time mark, maybe like 3.30 p.m.
Eastern.
We could do between 12 and 3 o'clock Eastern on Tuesday, or we could look at Wednesday from 3.30 to 5.00 Yeah.
What about 2.00 p.m.
Eastern?.00.00
2 p.m.
Eastern on Tuesday should be no problem.
I'll shoot that invite over.
Any resources that you guys want me to send your way?
I think just like the understanding of like ultimately view this as like we are hiring your team, right?
And so like we almost like we're not going to do eight rounds of interviews like you guys have done already, but we want to get a better understanding of that.
ultimately like that's the most important thing for us is like who will we be hiring and at what price, right?
Yeah, I think that's it.

And then like maybe kind of an expectation of like, I don't know, like what type of things we could work on or like what's been working for other clients, like doesn't have to be in follow-up materials, but for when we next chat, yeah, I think I'll send the SoW.
you.
So that you can review it before we have the next meeting, because that will give clarity on deliverables and work streams.

And if you feel like, you know, you need more clarity on a specific use case, or if there's something that's not represented in the SoW, well, then you'll be able to, like, mention it directly to Marcel.
So I will say a lot of what we would do would be editorial and refresh-focused, unless, like, we identified, like, a specific programmatic play in these conversations.
Like, that would be what the engagement would consist of.
I guess, is a programmatic play, are you guys doing that if we want to do it?
It's typically something that would be, like, a different work stream.
So it would increase, like, the price, because it would be beyond editorial.
But if you have something specific in mind, definitely let me know ahead of the next meeting, and I'll be able
Talk to Marcel about it.
He'll be able to tell you exactly what we'd be willing to do price-wise.
Okay, got it.
Have you thought about the programmatic play that you're looking to run?
Yeah, mean, it could potentially just be getting some help on not even programmatic stuff, but competitor landing pages, right?
Okay.
It could also be on use case pages of our product.
Like, we essentially kind of have, like, a Zapier workflow builder tool internally.
And so, like, having a content system that could help us churn out those pages quickly could be helpful.
Yeah, I think that's, like, two things I thought about.
Okay.
Yeah, I think those ideas sound in our wheelhouse.
For sure.
And I think it'll be exciting to connect with Marcel and figure out what we can do for you guys, given the work we've already done together and the context we have on your business.
Also, I think the product looks super cool as a salesperson.
I see a ton of use cases that would make my life easier.
So maybe we'll have that conversation, too.
Cool.
Awesome, guys.
Well, good meeting you.
And we'll chat again next Tuesday.
See you later.
Yeah, thanks.
Thank you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Reconnect Attention.com with GrowthX to explore potential re-engagement for AI-driven content marketing services.](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=3.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Attention.com is considering re-engaging with GrowthX due to increased AEO (AI Engine Optimization) inbound traffic](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=101.0)
  - [GrowthX has evolved its service offering, now including a platform component and a two-phase partnership approach](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=162.0)
  - [Pricing structure proposed: $20k for 8-week strategy sprint, followed by $12k/month for execution (12-month term)](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=131.0)
  - [Attention.com prefers a shorter commitment (3-month renewals) and potentially reduced strategy sprint fee](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=291.0)

## Topics

### Current State of GrowthX

  - [Still primarily service-driven, with platform access coming in \~2 months](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=158.0)
  - [New two-phase approach: strategy sprint followed by growth execution](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=197.0)
  - [Expanded team (80+ employees, $10M+ ARR) with specialized roles (managing editor, director, content manager)](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=942.0)
  - [Using Scrunch for AEO visibility, similar to Profound](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1278.0)

### Attention.com's Needs and Preferences

  - [Strong preference for "buy" over "build" for content marketing](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=284.0)
  - [Seeking managed service for article production and content refreshes](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=126.0)
  - [Interest in AEO-focused content structure (e.g., strategic H1/H2 usage, FAQs, metadata)](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=616.0)
  - [Desire for more creative and prescriptive approach compared to previous engagement](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=327.0)

### GrowthX's Enhanced Offering

  - [Focus on GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) and SEO hybrid approach](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=705.0)
  - [Comprehensive AI workflows for content creation, including deep research and fact-checking](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1372.0)
  - [Creation of detailed audience personas and company context artifacts](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1181.0)
  - [Ability to ingest client documentation and expert knowledge into AI workflows](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1399.0)

### Pricing and Engagement Structure

  - [Proposed: $20k for 8-week strategy sprint, $12k/month for execution (12-month term)](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=131.0)
  - [Attention.com prefers 3-month renewal terms and potentially reduced strategy sprint fee](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1544.0)
  - [GrowthX typically requires 12-month commitments due to high demand](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1627.0)

### Potential Additional Services

  - [Competitor landing page analysis](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1936.0)
  - [Use case pages for Attention.com's product (e.g., Zapier-like workflow builder tool)](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1951.0)

  - [Schedule follow-up meeting with Marcel (GrowthX founder) for Tuesday at 2 PM Eastern](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1779.0)
  - [GrowthX to send Statement of Work (SoW) for review before next meeting](https://fathom.video/share/kMKbkz1f4jSpJUuEtUSsfYRDUzvXMhGx?tab=summary&timestamp=1860.0)
